cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A system of government in which the executive (prime minister and cabinet) is drawn from and accountable to the legislature; the UK is the course example

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A system where the executive and legislature are separately elected with fixed terms; the executive is not removed by a legislative vote of no confidence; Mexico and Nigeria are course examples

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A hybrid system with a popularly elected president AND a prime minister accountable to the legislature; Russia is the course example

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A parliamentary mechanism by which the legislature can remove the prime minister and cabinet from power; the UK is the course example

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Iran's highest political authority, above the elected president; sets political agenda, commands the military, and appoints key government and judicial officials

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A legislature with a single chamber; China's NPC and Iran's Majles are the course examples

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A legislature with two distinct chambers (upper and lower); Mexico, Nigeria, Russia, and the UK are the course examples

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The small inner circle of China's Communist Party that holds the real governing power; constrains the NPC and controls key decisions behind closed doors

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Iran's elected unicameral parliament; approves legislation, budget, and cabinet nominees, but operates under oversight of the Guardian Council

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Iran's 12-member body — 6 Islamic jurists appointed by the Supreme Leader plus 6 civil lawyers — that vets candidates for office and reviews legislation for compliance with Islamic law

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Russia's lower house of parliament; 450 elected members; passes legislation and confirms the prime minister

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A legal restriction on how many times or how long an official may hold office; Mexico's president is limited to one six-year term

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A system where law is used as an instrument of state control rather than as a constraint on state power; characteristic of China under the CPC

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: China's unicameral national legislature; constitutionally described as the most powerful institution but in practice subordinate to the CPC; meets briefly once per year to ratify decisions

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Iran's body appointed by the Supreme Leader that resolves disputes between the Majles and the Guardian Council; also advises the Supreme Leader

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Iran's body of Islamic scholars that selects, oversees, and can remove the Supreme Leader

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Russia's upper house of parliament; members are appointed by regional governors and regional legislatures, not directly elected; approves budgets, treaties, judicial nominees, and troop deployments

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The UK's elected lower chamber; the prime minister must maintain its confidence; 650 members elected by first-past-the-post

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The UK's appointed upper chamber; reviews and amends Commons bills; cannot permanently block legislation, only delay it

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A feature of systems where executive authority is formally shared between two positions — in Russia, president and prime minister; in Iran, Supreme Leader and president

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The combining of executive and legislative authority in a parliamentary system so that the cabinet must maintain legislative confidence

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The time near the end of a term-limited executive's tenure when political power diminishes because re-election is impossible

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A formal legislative process to bring charges against a high government official; Nigeria's National Assembly can do this to the president by a two-thirds majority

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A formal legislative rebuke of a cabinet minister or government; weaker than removal but signals disapproval; a parliamentary-system check on the executive

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The power of a court to strike down laws or executive actions as unconstitutional; exists formally in Mexico, Nigeria, and Russia but is independently exercised only in Mexico and Nigeria

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The legal system based on judicial precedent (prior court decisions) rather than solely on written codes; used in the UK and a marker of the rule of law

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Islamic religious law that forms the basis of Iran's legal system and is applied in some northern Nigerian states under federalism

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A court system that makes rulings free from political pressure; its degree varies — strong in the UK, transitional in Mexico and Nigeria, weak in Russia, absent in China

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Mechanisms by which each branch of government can limit the powers of the other branches; stronger in democratic than authoritarian systems

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Informal term for a legislature that does not exercise independent power but merely approves decisions already made by the dominant executive or party — China's NPC is the course example

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: China's legislative body that acts in place of the full NPC most of the year; sets agenda, interprets laws, and supervises elections

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The role of a legislature in lending public credibility to executive decisions even when it lacks real independent power — a function performed by China's NPC

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The practice in UK Parliament where the prime minister and ministers must answer questions from elected members of Parliament — a parliamentary check on the executive

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: China's top military authority, whose chair position is held by the president; fuses party and military control in a single person

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The top leadership position within China's Communist Party, combined with the state presidency and the chairmanship of the Central Military Commission

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Nigeria's bicameral legislature composed of the Senate (108 members) and House of Representatives; both chambers approve legislation, and the Senate holds impeachment and confirmation powers

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The expectation that legislators vote in line with their party's official position; very strong in the UK's House of Commons, reinforcing executive dominance

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A government formed when no single party wins a parliamentary majority and two or more parties agree to govern together; possible in the UK's parliamentary system

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The institutional significance of China's NPC session in which the president's consolidation of the roles of General Secretary, state president, and Central Military Commission chair is formally ratified — illustrating how formal power structures differ from where actual decisions are made

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following BEST describes the relationship between China's National People's Congress (NPC) and the Politburo Standing Committee?

choices.text: The NPC holds real governing power because the constitution designates it as the country's most powerful institution
choices.explanation: Despite the constitutional claim, the NPC meets briefly once a year to ratify decisions already made; it is a legitimizing body, not the actual center of power.

choices.text: The Politburo Standing Committee is the actual center of power; the NPC rubber-stamps its decisions and legitimizes CPC rule
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.F.1a establishes the Politburo Standing Committee as the real decision-making center; the NPC serves a legitimizing function by formally approving CPC decisions.

choices.text: Both institutions share power equally, each checking the other
choices.explanation: There is no genuine power-sharing or check between these bodies; the NPC does not independently constrain the Politburo Standing Committee.

choices.text: The NPC is the upper chamber and the Politburo Standing Committee is the lower chamber of a bicameral system
choices.explanation: China has a unicameral legislature; the Politburo Standing Committee is a party body, not a legislative chamber.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The UK prime minister calls an election, loses her parliamentary majority, and a motion of no confidence passes. Which outcome BEST follows from this in a parliamentary system?

choices.text: The prime minister continues to serve until the fixed end of her term
choices.explanation: In a parliamentary system, the prime minister's tenure depends on maintaining a legislative majority; a successful no-confidence vote requires resignation or new elections.

choices.text: The prime minister and cabinet must resign or call new elections, because the executive cannot remain in office without legislative confidence
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.A.1 establishes that parliamentary systems allow the legislature to select and remove the head of government; a successful no-confidence vote removes the executive.

choices.text: The upper chamber (House of Lords) takes over executive functions temporarily
choices.explanation: The House of Lords has no executive power; it cannot assume government functions following a no-confidence vote.

choices.text: The monarch dissolves the legislature and rules directly
choices.explanation: The UK monarch is a ceremonial head of state; direct royal governance is not a feature of the modern UK parliamentary system.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: In Iran, the elected president oversees the civil service and conducts foreign policy but cannot appoint the head of the judiciary or set overall state policy. Which institution holds those powers?

choices.text: The Majles, as the elected legislature
choices.explanation: The Majles approves legislation and cabinet nominees, but it does not appoint the head of the judiciary or control overall state direction.

choices.text: The Supreme Leader, who outranks all elected institutions in Iran's theocratic structure
choices.explanation: Per CED PAU-3.C.2b, the Supreme Leader commands the military, appoints top ministers and the head of the judiciary, and sets overall state policy — all above the elected president.

choices.text: The Expediency Council, which has supreme legislative authority
choices.explanation: The Expediency Council resolves disputes between the Majles and the Guardian Council; it does not hold supreme executive authority.

choices.text: The Assembly of Experts, which governs daily state affairs
choices.explanation: The Assembly of Experts selects and oversees the Supreme Leader but does not govern daily state affairs.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A student argues that executive term limits always strengthen democracy by preventing authoritarian rule. Which of the following BEST identifies a weakness in this argument?

choices.text: Term limits have no effect on authoritarianism because authoritarian leaders never follow them
choices.explanation: This overstates the case; some authoritarian leaders do leave when term limits are enforced. The CED's actual concern is that term limits also carry costs.

choices.text: The CED notes that term limits also create lame-duck periods, impede policy continuity, and may force effective leaders out of office — suggesting they have both advantages and disadvantages
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.C.3 lists specific disadvantages of term limits alongside the advantages; the student's claim is one-sided and ignores the CED's balanced treatment.

choices.text: Term limits are prohibited in democratic systems because they restrict voter choice
choices.explanation: Term limits are used in many democracies; the CED does not say they are prohibited in democratic systems.

choices.text: Parliamentary systems always work better without term limits, so the argument is only relevant to presidential systems
choices.explanation: The CED discusses term limits across regime types; limiting the argument to presidential systems is not the CED's treatment.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following BEST distinguishes Russia's semi-presidential system from the UK's parliamentary system?

choices.text: Russia has a prime minister; the UK does not
choices.explanation: Both Russia and the UK have prime ministers; the distinction is in how the executive is accountable to the legislature, not whether a PM exists.

choices.text: In Russia, the president is popularly elected and can dismiss the prime minister without a parliamentary vote; in the UK, the prime minister is accountable to and drawn from Parliament
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.A.3 defines the semi-presidential system as having separate elections for president and legislature; PAU-3.A.1 establishes parliamentary systems as having the executive drawn from and accountable to the legislature.

choices.text: Russia holds no elections for either executive position; the UK holds elections for both
choices.explanation: Russia holds presidential elections; this is factually incorrect.

choices.text: Both Russia and the UK are parliamentary systems that differ only in their electoral rules
choices.explanation: Russia is a semi-presidential system, not parliamentary; the distinction is structural, not merely electoral.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following countries has a judiciary whose primary function is to ensure laws comply with Islamic religious law, with judges required to be trained in Sharia?

choices.text: Russia — the judicial system enforces Orthodox Christianity
choices.explanation: Russia's judiciary is not based on religious law; it is nominally secular and used as a tool of political control.

choices.text: Iran — the judiciary's major function is ensuring laws comply with Islamic Sharia, and the head of the judiciary is appointed by the Supreme Leader
choices.explanation: Per CED PAU-3.G.1b, Iran's judiciary enforces Islamic Sharia law; judges must be trained in Sharia and the judiciary is headed by a Supreme Leader appointee.

choices.text: Nigeria — Nigeria's entire national judiciary is based on Sharia law
choices.explanation: Nigeria applies Sharia law only in some northern states under the federal system; the national judiciary is not Sharia-based.

choices.text: China — the CPC requires communist ideology to inform all court decisions
choices.explanation: China's judiciary operates under 'rule by law' with CPC control, but it is not based on religious law.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following BEST describes how Nigeria can remove its president from office?

choices.text: A vote of no confidence by the House of Representatives only
choices.explanation: A vote of no confidence is a parliamentary mechanism; Nigeria is a presidential system. Removal requires impeachment by both chambers of the National Assembly.

choices.text: The National Assembly (both chambers by two-thirds majority) can impeach the president for gross misconduct
choices.explanation: Per CED PAU-3.D.1, Nigeria uses impeachment through a two-thirds majority of both legislative chambers as the mechanism for executive removal.

choices.text: The Supreme Leader can remove the president at any time
choices.explanation: Nigeria has no Supreme Leader; this structure belongs to Iran.

choices.text: The military can remove the president through a constitutional provision
choices.explanation: Nigeria's constitution does not authorize military removal; Nigeria's past military coups were extra-constitutional acts.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following BEST explains why Russia's Federation Council is less democratically accountable than its State Duma?

choices.text: The Federation Council has more legislative power than the Duma
choices.explanation: The Federation Council approves budgets and treaties but does not control ordinary legislation the way the Duma does; this does not explain democratic accountability differences.

choices.text: The Federation Council is appointed by regional governors and legislatures, not directly elected by citizens
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.E.1e establishes that the Federation Council is composed of appointed members, not directly elected ones — making it less democratically accountable than the elected Duma.

choices.text: The Federation Council is unicameral and the Duma is bicameral
choices.explanation: Both are chambers of Russia's legislature; this description is confused. Together they form the bicameral Federal Assembly.

choices.text: The Federation Council members are elected for life terms, insulating them from voters
choices.explanation: Federation Council members are appointed by regional authorities, not elected for life terms.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Iran's Guardian Council vetoes Majles legislation as incompatible with Islamic law. This BEST illustrates which concept?

choices.text: Legislative independence — the Guardian Council acts as an independent check on the executive
choices.explanation: The Guardian Council constrains the legislature (Majles), not the executive; and it is an unelected body appointed in part by the Supreme Leader, not an independent democratic check.

choices.text: Legislative constraint by an unelected institution — the Guardian Council limits Majles independence in the name of Islamic law compliance
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.F.1d describes the Guardian Council as constraining legislative independence by vetting candidates and reviewing laws for Islamic compliance.

choices.text: Judicial review — the Guardian Council acts like a constitutional court striking down laws
choices.explanation: While the function resembles judicial review, the Guardian Council is not a court; it is an unelected clerical body that enforces religious, not constitutional, standards.

choices.text: Federalism — the Guardian Council represents regional interests against the central parliament
choices.explanation: The Guardian Council is not a federalism mechanism; Iran is a unitary state and the Guardian Council operates at the national level on religious grounds.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following BEST identifies an advantage of executive term limits according to the CED?

choices.text: Term limits guarantee that the most competent leader remains in office
choices.explanation: The CED lists forcing good executives to leave as a disadvantage of term limits, not an advantage.

choices.text: Term limits check executive power and inhibit the emergence of dictators and personality rule
choices.explanation: Per CED PAU-3.C.3a, a named advantage of term limits is that they check executive power and prevent the consolidation of authority into dictatorial hands.

choices.text: Term limits eliminate lame-duck periods, ensuring policy momentum
choices.explanation: The CED lists lame-duck periods as a disadvantage of term limits, not an advantage.

choices.text: Term limits increase policy continuity by training experienced leaders over many terms
choices.explanation: Term limits are said by the CED to impede policy continuity and prevent building executive experience — these are listed disadvantages.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following accurately describes a key difference between Mexico's and Nigeria's executive systems?

choices.text: Mexico has a parliamentary system; Nigeria has a presidential system
choices.explanation: Both Mexico and Nigeria have presidential systems; neither is parliamentary.

choices.text: Mexico's president is limited to one six-year term; Nigeria's president serves up to two four-year terms
choices.explanation: Per CED PAU-3.C.2c and 3.C.2d, Mexico restricts its president to a single sexenio term; Nigeria's constitution permits re-election for a second term.

choices.text: Mexico's president serves as head of state only; Nigeria's president serves as both head of state and head of government
choices.explanation: Both Mexico's and Nigeria's presidents serve as head of state AND head of government per the CED.

choices.text: Nigeria uses a vote of no confidence to remove its president; Mexico uses impeachment
choices.explanation: Votes of no confidence apply to parliamentary systems; Nigeria uses impeachment (presidential system). Mexico has an extreme rarity of impeachment proceedings.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following statements BEST describes judicial independence in Russia?

choices.text: Russia's judiciary is fully independent because the constitution gives courts the power of judicial review
choices.explanation: While Russia's constitution formally grants judicial review, the CED notes this power has not been used to limit governing branches; constitutional text does not equal functional independence.

choices.text: Russia's government uses the judicial system to target political opposition; formal judicial review exists but is not exercised independently
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.G.1g explicitly states that Russia's government uses the judicial system against opposition and that formal judicial review does not constrain governing branches in practice.

choices.text: Russia has no formal judiciary; criminal matters are decided by the Duma
choices.explanation: Russia has a functioning formal judicial system; the issue is its lack of independence, not its absence.

choices.text: Russia's judiciary is as independent as the UK's because both countries have constitutions
choices.explanation: Having a constitution does not guarantee judicial independence; the CED presents the UK and Russia as opposite ends of the judicial independence spectrum.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The degree of the judiciary's freedom from political interference, determined by factors including how judges are appointed, their tenure length, their professional qualifications, and the process to remove them

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: China's head of government, nominated by the president and confirmed by the NPC; oversees the civil service and State Council

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The ceremonial or formal representative of a country; in the UK this is the monarch; in Iran the Supreme Leader outranks this role

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The leader who runs day-to-day executive operations; the prime minister in the UK and Russia; the president in Mexico and Nigeria

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The highest authority over a country's armed forces; in most course countries this is the president; in Iran it is the Supreme Leader

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The ability to maintain consistent long-term policies across administrations; executive term limits can disrupt this by forcing leaders to leave before completing policy goals

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Mexico's lower house of Congress; 500 members total — 300 elected in single-member districts and 200 by proportional representation; approves legislation and levies taxes

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: In which of the following countries do leadership changes occur 'behind closed doors' through party mechanisms rather than public legislative proceedings?

choices.text: Mexico — the president appoints a successor through el dedazo
choices.explanation: El dedazo was abolished as part of Mexico's democratic transition; this describes a historical practice not a structural constitutional feature.

choices.text: China — leadership changes are accomplished through CPC internal mechanisms, not public elections or legislative proceedings
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.C.2a explicitly states that changes in China's top leadership are accomplished behind closed doors within the CPC.

choices.text: UK — the monarch secretly appoints the next prime minister
choices.explanation: The UK monarch formally appoints the party leader with the most Commons seats as PM; this is a formal constitutional procedure, not a secret process.

choices.text: Nigeria — the National Assembly votes in secret on presidential succession
choices.explanation: Nigeria's presidential elections are public; succession through impeachment proceedings is a formal constitutional process, not a secret mechanism.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following BEST explains why independent judiciaries strengthen democratic governance?

choices.text: Independent judges earn higher salaries, attracting more qualified candidates
choices.explanation: Judicial compensation is not the CED's basis for explaining why independent judiciaries strengthen democracy.

choices.text: Independent judiciaries maintain checks and balances, protect rights and liberties, establish the rule of law, and maintain separation of powers
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.H.2 directly lists these four functions as the reason independent judiciaries strengthen democratic governance.

choices.text: Independent judiciaries always support the ruling party's agenda, providing policy stability
choices.explanation: An independent judiciary does the opposite — it can rule against the ruling party when laws or executive actions are unconstitutional.

choices.text: Independent judiciaries eliminate the need for legislative oversight of the executive
choices.explanation: Independent judiciaries operate alongside, not in place of, legislative oversight; checks and balances require multiple independent institutions.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following countries uses its Supreme Court as the final court of appeals for devolution disputes between the national parliament and regional assemblies?

choices.text: Russia — the Constitutional Court resolves disputes between federal and regional governments
choices.explanation: While Russia has a Constitutional Court, the CED's specific example of a court resolving devolution disputes is the UK Supreme Court.

choices.text: United Kingdom — the Supreme Court protects human rights and rules on devolution disputes between Westminster and devolved assemblies
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.G.1i specifies that the UK Supreme Court serves as the final appeals court, protects rights, and rules on devolution disputes.

choices.text: Iran — the Guardian Council resolves disputes between the national and provincial governments
choices.explanation: The Guardian Council vets candidates and checks legislation for Islamic compliance; it does not resolve devolution disputes. Iran is a unitary state with no devolved regional assemblies.

choices.text: Mexico — the Supreme Court resolves conflicts between federal and state constitutions
choices.explanation: Mexico does have judicial review but the CED's specific devolution dispute example is the UK, not Mexico.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: An upper legislative chamber whose members are not directly elected; Russia's Federation Council and the UK's House of Lords are the course examples

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The permanent, non-partisan body of government employees who implement policy; the head of government typically oversees this body in all course countries

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A scholar argues that parliamentary systems give the executive unchecked power because the prime minister controls the majority in parliament. Which of the following BEST refutes this argument?

choices.text: Parliamentary prime ministers are always weaker than presidents because they lack a direct popular mandate
choices.explanation: The argument about popular mandate does not directly address the 'unchecked power' claim, and it overstates a real but contested point.

choices.text: Parliamentary systems have meaningful checks: the legislature can vote no confidence, censure ministers, question the PM, and refuse legislation; the PM must maintain majority support continuously
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.B.2 specifically lists these parliamentary checks — no-confidence votes, censure, question time, deadline setting — as constraints on the executive in parliamentary systems.

choices.text: Parliamentary systems eliminate the executive branch entirely, making it impossible to have unchecked executive power
choices.explanation: Parliamentary systems have an executive branch; they simply combine legislative and executive accountability more closely than presidential systems.

choices.text: Parliamentary systems always have coalition governments that limit the PM's power
choices.explanation: Parliamentary systems do not always produce coalitions; single-party majorities are common (e.g., UK Conservatives with strong majorities). Coalitions are contingent, not universal.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which country has a judiciary in which judges must be trained in Islamic Sharia law AND the head of the judiciary is appointed by an unelected religious authority?

choices.text: Nigeria
choices.explanation: Nigeria applies Sharia law in some northern states under federalism, but its national judiciary is not Sharia-based and the national judicial appointment process differs from Iran's.

choices.text: Iran
choices.explanation: Per CED PAU-3.G.1b, Iran's judiciary must ensure legal compliance with Islamic Sharia, judges must be trained in Sharia, and the head of the judiciary is appointed by the Supreme Leader.

choices.text: Russia
choices.explanation: Russia's judiciary is not Sharia-based; it operates on secular civil law under CPC-style political control.

choices.text: United Kingdom
choices.explanation: The UK judiciary uses common law, not religious law, and operates under the rule of law independent of any religious authority.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A foundational legal document outlining the structure, powers, and limits of government; all six course countries have one, though enforcement and the degree of actual constraint it places on leaders varies widely

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Nigeria's constitution establishes Sharia courts in its northern states. Which of the following BEST explains how this is possible within Nigeria's national constitutional framework?

choices.text: Nigeria is a unitary state, so the national government can impose any judicial system it chooses on all regions
choices.explanation: Nigeria is a federal state; regional authority is constitutionally defined, allowing northern states to adopt Sharia courts within the federal system.

choices.text: Nigeria's federal system allows subnational units to establish their own judicial arrangements, including Sharia courts for Muslim-majority northern states
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.G.1e notes that under Nigerian federalism, Sharia courts have been established in northern states; this reflects the local autonomy that federal structures permit.

choices.text: Nigeria is classified as a theocracy, so Sharia applies nationally
choices.explanation: Nigeria is not a theocracy; it is a federal presidential democracy with hybrid features. Sharia courts are a sub-national feature under federalism.

choices.text: The Guardian Council in Nigeria approves the use of Sharia courts
choices.explanation: The Guardian Council is an Iranian institution; Nigeria has no Guardian Council.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following BEST illustrates why an independent legislature can reinforce political legitimacy?

choices.text: An independent legislature always passes every bill submitted by the executive
choices.explanation: An independent legislature that always passes executive bills is not genuinely independent; this describes a rubber-stamp body.

choices.text: An independent legislature can respond to public demand, openly debate policy, facilitate compromise, and restrict executive overreach — showing citizens their interests are represented
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.F.2 lists these functions — responding to demand, open debate, facilitating compromise, extending civil liberties, restricting executive power — as how independent legislatures reinforce legitimacy.

choices.text: Legitimacy is enhanced when the executive controls the legislature, because unified government is more effective
choices.explanation: While unified government can be efficient, the CED links legitimacy to legislative independence and responsiveness to public demand, not executive control of the legislature.

choices.text: Legislatures only reinforce legitimacy in authoritarian states by providing an illusion of representation
choices.explanation: Independent legislatures reinforce legitimacy in democratic systems by genuinely representing interests; the illusion concept applies to authoritarian legislatures that lack independence.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Mexico's president nominates Supreme Court magistrates who serve 15-year terms and must be confirmed by the Senate. Which factor in this process BEST enhances judicial independence?

choices.text: Presidential nomination of judges weakens independence because the president controls the judiciary
choices.explanation: Presidential nomination is only one factor; the subsequent Senate confirmation and the long tenure both mitigate executive control. The CED presents Mexico's judiciary as 'in transition' toward independence.

choices.text: The 15-year fixed term insulates judges from immediate political pressure by reducing the frequency of reappointment decisions
choices.explanation: Per CED PAU-3.H.1, longer judicial terms are a factor that increases independence by reducing how often judges depend on political actors for reappointment.

choices.text: Senate confirmation undermines independence because the Senate can remove judges at any time
choices.explanation: Senate confirmation is an initial check on the nomination process, not an ongoing removal power; confirmed judges serve fixed terms.

choices.text: Judicial independence is irrelevant in presidential systems because the president already separates executive and legislative power
choices.explanation: Separation of powers between executive and legislative branches does not automatically produce judicial independence; the judiciary's independence from both branches depends on appointment and tenure structures.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Chinese leader who consolidated the roles of General Secretary of the CPC, state president, and chair of the Central Military Commission — combining party, state, and military authority in a single person as described in CED PAU-3.C.2a

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A body of senior ministers or advisers who advise the chief executive and head government departments; in parliamentary systems this body is drawn from and accountable to the legislature

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: The head of government in a parliamentary or semi-presidential system; in the UK, drawn from the majority party in the House of Commons

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A passage describes a country whose legislature formally has the most governmental power but in practice meets once a year to approve decisions already made by a small committee of party leaders. Which country BEST matches this description?

choices.text: United Kingdom
choices.explanation: The UK's House of Commons meets year-round and exercises genuine legislative power with real debates, votes of no confidence, and the ability to remove the government.

choices.text: China
choices.explanation: China's NPC is constitutionally described as the most powerful institution but in practice meets briefly once a year; real power is exercised by the Politburo Standing Committee.

choices.text: Nigeria
choices.explanation: Nigeria's National Assembly exercises genuine legislative power in a presidential system; it is not a rubber-stamp legislature.

choices.text: Mexico
choices.explanation: Mexico's bicameral Congress exercises genuine legislative independence as part of the country's democratic transition; it does not rubber-stamp party decisions.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following is a key difference between Mexico's Chamber of Deputies and Mexico's Senate?

choices.text: Only the Chamber of Deputies holds any legislative power; the Senate is ceremonial
choices.explanation: Both chambers hold legislative power; the distinction is in their unique powers, not in whether either holds power at all.

choices.text: The Chamber of Deputies approves legislation and levies taxes; the Senate has the unique power to confirm Supreme Court appointments and approve treaties
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.E.1c specifies these distinct powers: the Chamber of Deputies on legislation and taxation; the Senate on Supreme Court confirmation, treaty approval, and federal intervention in states.

choices.text: The Chamber of Deputies is appointed; the Senate is elected
choices.explanation: Both chambers of Mexico's Congress are elected; no appointments system applies to Mexico's legislature.

choices.text: The Senate is unicameral while the Chamber of Deputies is bicameral
choices.explanation: This confuses the distinction; both are chambers within Mexico's single bicameral Congress. A Senate cannot itself be bicameral.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Nigeria's upper legislative chamber; three senators per state = 108 total; holds impeachment powers and confirms presidential nominations

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Mexico's upper house; 128 members; confirms Supreme Court appointments, approves treaties, and authorizes federal intervention in state matters

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Which of the following BEST describes what makes Iran's executive different from Mexico's and Nigeria's, both of which are also presidential systems?

choices.text: Iran's president has far more power than Mexico's or Nigeria's because they serve unlimited terms
choices.explanation: Iran's president is limited to two four-year terms and has less actual power than appears, not more.

choices.text: Iran has a Supreme Leader above its elected president who sets overall political agenda and commands the military — making Iran's executive dual and clerical in character
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.C.2b establishes that the Supreme Leader outranks Iran's elected president; Iran's dual executive has a clerical dimension absent in Mexico and Nigeria.

choices.text: Iran uses a parliamentary system, not a presidential system
choices.explanation: Iran holds separate presidential elections, distinguishing it from parliamentary systems; however, the Supreme Leader's authority makes it a theocratic hybrid, not a pure parliamentary system either.

choices.text: Iran's judiciary, not its executive, is the most powerful institution
choices.explanation: The Supreme Leader controls the judiciary as well; judicial power flows from and is subordinate to clerical authority.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: What institutional role does the Expediency Council in Iran serve that makes it distinct from the Guardian Council?

choices.text: The Expediency Council vets candidates for office; the Guardian Council resolves legislative disputes
choices.explanation: This reverses the institutions' roles; the Guardian Council vets candidates, and the Expediency Council resolves disputes.

choices.text: The Expediency Council resolves disputes between the Majles and the Guardian Council; it does not vet candidates or review legislation for Islamic compliance
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.F.1c and PAU-3.F.1d distinguish the two bodies: the Guardian Council vets candidates and reviews laws; the Expediency Council is the dispute-resolution body appointed by the Supreme Leader.

choices.text: The Expediency Council selects and can remove the Supreme Leader
choices.explanation: The Assembly of Experts selects and can remove the Supreme Leader; the Expediency Council performs an advisory and dispute-resolution function.

choices.text: Both the Expediency Council and the Guardian Council perform identical functions in Iran's legislative oversight
choices.explanation: They have distinct, specified roles per the CED; conflating them is a common misconception.

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: A senior body with the power to make binding decisions for a state; one of the formal institutions through which states exercise sovereignty

cards.subject: ap-comparative-government
cards.prompt: Mexico's Supreme Court has the power of judicial review and magistrates serve 15-year terms. Nigeria's Supreme Court also has judicial review but faces ongoing challenges to its independence from political pressure. Which of the following BEST explains this difference?

choices.text: Mexico is a unitary state, giving its Supreme Court more authority than Nigeria's federal system allows
choices.explanation: Mexico is a federal state; the difference in judicial independence is not explained by the federal/unitary distinction.

choices.text: Mexico's judiciary is further along in its transition toward independence, while Nigeria is still working to reduce corruption and establish institutional independence
choices.explanation: CED PAU-3.G.1c describes Mexico's judiciary as 'in transition' toward independence; PAU-3.G.1e notes Nigeria is working to reestablish independence and reduce corruption — both are transitional, but at different stages.

choices.text: Nigeria's judiciary does not have judicial review; only Mexico's does
choices.explanation: Both countries have formal judicial review per the CED; the difference is in the degree of independence with which courts exercise that power.

choices.text: Mexico's judiciary is Sharia-based, giving it clearer rules than Nigeria's secular system
choices.explanation: Mexico's judiciary is not Sharia-based; it operates under civil law with constitutional judicial review.
